questions.question: What is the definition of an improper fraction?
options: A fraction whose numerator is strictly less than the denominator.
A fraction whose numerator is greater than or equal to the denominator.
A fraction that has a zero in the denominator.
A fraction that cannot be simplified.
questions.explanation: An improper fraction has a top number (numerator) that is greater than or equal to the bottom number (denominator), representing 1 or more wholes.

questions.question: Which of the following is an improper fraction?
questions.explanation: In $\frac{9}{8}$, the numerator (9) is greater than the denominator (8), making it improper. All other options have numerators smaller than their denominators.

questions.question: Which of these fractions is equal to exactly 1 whole?
questions.explanation: A fraction where the numerator is equal to the denominator (and not zero) is equal to 1 whole, since all pieces of the divided unit are selected.

questions.question: Which statement is true regarding the value of an improper fraction?
options: It is always strictly less than 1.
It is always equal to or greater than 1.
It is always a decimal between 0 and 1.
questions.explanation: Because the numerator is greater than or equal to the denominator, the value is always greater than or equal to 1 whole.

questions.question: If you have 11 pieces of bread, and each whole loaf is cut into 3 equal pieces, what fraction of a loaf do you have?
questions.explanation: Each loaf is divided into 3 parts (denominator = 3), and you have 11 parts (numerator = 11), giving you the improper fraction $\frac{11}{3}$.
